title-group.article-title: Resolving Isomeric Structures in Natural Complex Carbohydrates: A Comparative Study of SLIM and Cyclic Ion Mobility Platforms

article-meta.aff: 2 PROBE Research Infrastructure, BIBS Facility, INRAE, Nantes, France
3 Biological Sciences Division, Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, Richland, Washington, USA

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 Battelle Memorial Institute and The Author(s). Rapid Communications in Mass Spectrometry published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Rationale
sec.p: Recent advances in ion mobility mass spectrometry (IMS) have led to the development of high‐resolution platforms such as cyclic IMS and SLIM IMS (structures for lossless ion manipulations), both offering enhanced capabilities for resolving complex biomolecular conformations.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: In this study, these two IMS technologies were evaluated for the characterization of complex natural polysaccharides, using homogalacturonan oligosaccharides (HGOS) derived from plant cell wall pectin as a model system. HGOS were generated through enzymatic depolymerization of the parent polymer and analyzed on both platforms. This approach enabled, for the first time, a direct comparison of mass spectra and ion mobility maps for identical species with varying degrees of polymerization (DP) and methylation (DM).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: As expected, single‐pass analyses on the 10‐m SLIM IMS provided higher resolving power, allowing a greater number of IMS features to be separated for a given species (e.g., DP5DM4) compared to single‐pass cyclic IMS experiments. However, IMS features unresolved in cyclic IMS single‐pass mode could be accessed through multi‐pass separations. A key distinction between the platforms lies in ion formation and charge state distributions. The SLIM IMS, retrofitted to an Agilent 6560, utilizes a commercial ion source and ion optics that result in the formation of larger and higher charged species, with more cation adducts. While beneficial for resolution, this distribution can dilute signal intensity across multiple charge states, impacting sensitivity. In contrast, the cyclic IMS employs a Z‐spray source that favors lower charge states, facilitating more straightforward diagnostic interpretation and characterization. Despite the superior intrinsic IMS resolution of the 10‐m SLIM, the combination of ionization behavior, instrument tuning requirements, and data processing complexity makes the cyclic IMS more practical for routine analysis.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Both IMS platforms demonstrated strong capabilities for resolving HGOS structures, highlighting their complementarity and effectiveness in studying complex polysaccharide mixtures.
Keywords: carbohydrate, cyclic, IMS, oligogalacturonan, SLIM
Revised 2026 Mar 18; Received 2026 Jan 12; Accepted 2026 Mar 21; Issue date 2026 Aug 15.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Polysaccharides are highly diverse biopolymers found in algae, fungi, plants, and microorganisms, whose structural complexity arises from variations in monosaccharide composition, linkage types, branching patterns, and chemical modifications [1]. This complexity is further intensified by multiple forms of isomerism that can occur either at the constitutive monomer level (epimerism and tautomerism) or at the poly‐/oligosaccharide level with the branching between subunits (regioisomerism and α/β anomerism) [2, 3, 4, 5]. Such structural diversity is strongly linked to the wide variety of biological properties of polysaccharides, from cell signalling, water uptake, or plant defense to key structural functions, as in cell walls [6, 7]. Even subtle structural modifications can significantly influence those biological functions, highlighting the need for tailored approaches to elucidate the structure–function relationship. For instance, cellulose and mixed‐linked β‐glucans, although both composed of glucose subunits yet connected differently, exhibit distinct roles in plant cell walls. Cellulose chains assemble into rigid microfibrils providing mechanical strength and resistance to chemical or enzymatic degradation [8], while mixed‐linked β‐glucans interconnect these microfibrils, forming a flexible network that modulates wall extensibility and porosity, particularly during active cell expansion [9].

Among plant polysaccharides, homogalacturonans (HGs) represent a particularly challenging class to characterize. HGs are the most abundant pectic polysaccharides in cell walls (more than 65%), where they act as matrix components interconnecting and surrounding cellulose microfibrils, and consist of linear chains of α(1–4)‐D‐galacturonic acid (GalA) residues with DP values of 30‐100 [10]. Although their backbone may appear simple, HGs exhibit significant heterogeneity due to the variations in the degree of methylation (DM) and in the distribution of these methyl‐esterification along the backbone. These differences strongly impact the properties of the polysaccharides. Indeed, the pattern and degree of methylation critically determine the physicochemical behavior of HGs, particularly their ability to form calcium‐mediated gels that regulate wall rigidity in planta and support their gelling capacity in food and industrial applications [11]. Therefore, developing tailored analytical approaches is essential for the comprehensive characterization of HGs, including their methyl‐esterification pattern.

Ion mobility spectrometry (IMS) combined with mass spectrometry (MS) has emerged as a powerful tool to address the challenges posed by polysaccharides' heterogeneity and in particular, their isomerism, a level of structural detail that is difficult to attain using MS alone. Indeed, MS offers valuable features such as high sensitivity, selectivity, and the ability to provide detailed structural information. In contrast, IMS can separate isomeric species when they adopt distinct gas phase structures [12].

However, resolving isomeric carbohydrates generally requires high IMS resolving power. In ion mobility, the resolving power (Rp) expresses the ability to distinguish ions of similar mobilities and is defined from the arrival time distribution (ATD) as the ratio of the drift time to the peak width at half maximum Rp=td/Δt1/2, or equivalently from the CCS distribution as Rp=CCS/ΔCCS, where td is the drift time and Δt1/2 the FWHM of the ATD peak. Dodds et al. [13] showed that baseline separation of constitutional isomers (e.g., regioisomers or tautomers) typically requires a resolving power of ~100, while separation of stereoisomers (e.g., anomers or epimers) may require Rp values exceeding 500. Such resolving powers are generally beyond the capabilities of conventional drift tube IMS and early short‐path TWIMS implementations (e.g., ~25 cm Synapt‐type platforms).

Yet, recent technological advances have led to such high‐resolution instruments, notably the commercial Cyclic IMS (cIMS, Waters Corp., Wilmslow, United Kingdom) presented by Giles et al. [14], which are capable of Rp > 900 [3]; and Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM), developed by R.D. Smith and colleagues [15, 16], which can reach Rp values exceeding 1800 in multi‐pass experiments (resolving power in excess of ~300 for a single pass of 13‐m pathlength) [16]. SLIM has been commercialized by MOBILion, which released the MOBIE platform featuring a single pass with a 13‐m serpentine path length [17].

The cyclic IMS device employs travelling wave ion mobility spectroscopy (TWIMS) and allows ions to perform multiple passes in a 98‐cm circular racetrack, enhancing their separation. In the configuration of this instrument, ion mobility can be studied either on intact parent ions or on their fragments. Fragment ions can be studied by IMS after their generation by collision‐induced dissociation (CID) in a trap cell located before the IMS racetrack, or in a pre‐array store cell, where ions selected after a first mobility separation can be further fragmented. This setup enables the so‐called multistage IMS experiments (IMSn).

Cyclic IMS has proven particularly valuable in glycosciences, resolving challenging carbohydrate structures [3, 5, 18, 19, 20], including α/β carbohydrate anomers [18], branched N‐glycan isomers [21], and complex human milk oligosaccharide mixtures [5]. In line with these developments, our group has also pioneered the use of cyclic IMS to address carbohydrate isomerism and structural characterization challenges, providing insights into monosaccharide structures, as well as linear or branched complex polysaccharides [22, 23, 24, 25, 26]. These studies were conducted predominantly on complex natural samples rather than on commercial standards, and they demonstrated that all types of carbohydrate isomerism can be resolved using the cyclic IMS platform.

SLIM, which also relies on a TWIMS‐based strategy, features typically an extended 13‐m ion path, in a serpentine configuration, allowing ion confinement and separation over multiple passes with exceptional high resolution. SLIM has been extensively applied to glycan analysis over the past few years, particularly in combination with cryogenic infrared action spectroscopy (cryo‐IR) [27, 28, 29], and on‐board CID [28] by Rizzo's group. They have demonstrated its utility in deciphering glycan mixtures [4, 29], providing their detailed structural insights, although such applications have become less frequent in recent literature.

On the other hand, SLIM—as originally developed at PNNL (in configuration featuring the 13‐m serpentine path or other path lengths)—has been heavily applied to the separation of diverse classes of isomeric structures, although rarely to glycans. Notable applications include the differentiation of tryptic peptide epimers [29], steroid metabolites [30], and dicaffeoylquinic acids [16]. More recently, SLIM has also been employed to analyze complex biological samples such as brain total lipid extracts in both positive and negative ion modes [31], antibody‐drug conjugates [32], phosphopeptides [33], and native‐like proteins [34].

Thus, although both cyclic IMS and SLIM IMS have demonstrated their ability to elucidate the structures of isomeric glycans, to the best of our knowledge, no direct comparison between these two high‐resolution IMS platforms has been reported on the same sample. In addition, previous studies using SLIM have focused exclusively on purified oligosaccharides, leaving its performance on natural complex mixtures of carbohydrates unexplored. These gaps motivated our present study, where we directly compare, for the first time, cyclic IMS and SLIM IMS (a not‐commercial 10‐m configuration with multi‐pass capacity implemented at PNNL was used in this study [35]) for their capacity to separate isomeric structures of homogalacturonans (HGs). We used HGs as a model to illustrate the difficulties encountered when resolving oligosaccharide structures using conventional MS approaches.

sec.title: Chemicals and Oligosaccharides
sec.p: The sample used in this study was the same as that employed in our previous study [36]. Its synthesis and characterization are detailed in Ralet et al. [37]. It was produced from citrus‐derived homogalacturonans (69% methylated, HGB69) through enzymatic digestion with a pectin lyase (PL, EC 4.2.2.10), yielding a chemically diverse mixture of oligogalacturonans with varying degrees of polymerization (DP) and methylation (DM). For all experiments, the sample was infused at 250 μg/mL in a 50:50 (v/v) H2O/MeOH solution.
HPLC‐grade methanol (MeOH) and water (H2O) used for cyclic IMS experiments were purchased from Carlo‐Erba (Val de Reuil, France).
HPLC‐grade methanol (MeOH) and water (H2O) used for 10‐m SLIM IMS experiments were purchased from Sigma‐Aldrich.

sec.title: Cyclic IMS‐MS Experiments
sec.p: Cyclic IMS analyses were performed on a SELECT SERIES Cyclic IMS (cIMS) instrument (Waters, Wilmslow, United Kingdom). The sample was infused at a concentration of 250 μg/mL in 50:50 H2O/MeOH through a syringe pump set at a flow rate of 5 μL/min.

The Z‐spray ESI source parameters were set as follows: the capillary voltage at 2.2 kV, the cone voltage at 100 V, the source temperature at 100°C, the desolvation temperature at 450°C, and the nebulizer pressure at 3 bar. IMS separations were performed in static mode with a cyclic and array traveling wave (TW) velocity both at 370 m/s and a TW static height of 18 V. The IMS‐MS parameters are detailed in Text S1.
In the present configuration, one cycle of the cyclic IMS corresponds to an effective pathlength of approximately 1 m.

sec.title: SLIM IMS‐MS Experiments
sec.p: SLIM experiments were performed on Agilent 6560 which was retrofitted with a 10‐m SLIM device [35]. Samples were infused using the commercial AJS ESI source at a flow rate of 10 μL/min and concentrations of 250 μg/mL. The capillary voltage was held at 4 kV, the drying gas was set at 350°C and a flow rate of 5 L/min, the nebulizer was set to 20 psi, and the sheath gas temperature and flow rate were 275°C and 12 L/min. IMS separations were performed using a TW with a square profile, speed of 208 m/s, amplitude of 35Vpp, and a guard voltage of −25 V. Prior to the IMS separation ions were accumulated on the SLIM device for 10 ms while the TW amplitude was set to 5 Vpp. After the 10‐ms fill time, the TW amplitude was changed to 35Vpp to begin the separations. RF voltages for SLIM were set to ~300 Vpp at 0.95 MHz. Single‐pass SLIM IMS separations in this study correspond to an effective pathlength of 10 m.

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: Cyclic IMS data were recorded using the Quartz software on the cIMS instrument (Waters Embedded Analyser release 9, Waters, Wilmslow, United Kingdom). Data processing was performed on Masslynx v4.2 and Driftscope v2.9 (both from Waters, Wilmslow, United Kingdom). ATDs smoothing was performed using the Savitzky–Golay algorithm (window size = 1, number of smooths = 2).
10‐m SLIM IMS data were visualized, and the arrival times of the calibrants and analytes were extracted using the in‐house developed software tool (https://github.com/PNNL‐CompMass‐Spec/UIMF‐Viewer).

The Rp was calculated as the ratio of the drift time to the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the corresponding peak in the ATD spectrum, while the peak‐to‐peak resolution (Rp‐p) between two adjacent peaks was determined from the difference in their drift times divided by the sum of their FWHMs.

sec.title: High‐Resolution IMS (SLIM IMS and Cyclic IMS) Outperforms UPLC for the Separation of Complex Chemically Diverse Mixtures
sec.p: A mixture of chemically diverse carbohydrates produced by enzymatic hydrolysis of homogalacturonan was analyzed on both 10‐m SLIM IMS and cyclic IMS platforms. Our aim was to perform the IMS experiments under optimal conditions (see “Experimental Section”) in a single pass on the ion mobility racetracks (i.e., 10 m for SLIM IMS and ~1 m for cyclic IMS). Of note, we are comparing completely different instrumentations; thus, the optimal conditions are not similar for both instruments.

The sample was produced from a digestion of a 69% methylated homogalacturonans (HGB69) with a lyase, resulting in chemically diverse oligogalacturonans with various DP and DM. A representation of such oligogalacturonan structure—with a DP of 5 and a DM of 4—is provided in Figure 1. The ions observed in both 10‐m SLIM IMS and cyclic IMS experiments correspond predominantly to deprotonated oligogalacturonans [M‐H]−, along with the presence of sodium adducts [M + Na‐2H]−, whose relative abundance varies depending on the ion source conditions. It is also worth noting that these oligogalacturonan mixtures from HGB69 digestion have been previously characterized within our group, using LC–MS/MS [36]. The mobility distributions reported here were obtained by direct infusion of the same sample, without chromatographic separations.

caption.p: Oligo‐homogalacturonan structure (A) represented in a DP5DM4 methylated species with its simplified (B) SNFG representation that shows the possible methylation positions.

sec.p: The total MS spectra (Figure S1) from both experiments are highly congested due to the presence of various DP species with multiple DM and multiple charge states. The IMS dimension allows discrimination among different charge states and resolves different species.

Figure 2 shows the IMS heatmaps obtained from both IMS platforms with the drift times and the reconstructed MS spectra of the identified species in selected regions of the heatmaps. In addition, Table S1 and Table S2 summarize all the identified species and the nature of the detected ions for each spectrum.

caption.p: IMS‐MS experiments of HGB69 using 13‐m SLIM IMS vs. cyclic IMS. 2D IMS heatmaps from (A) 13‐m SLIM IMS and (B) cyclic IMS experiments showing the arrival time distributions (ATDs) and the corresponding m/z values of the detected HG species. (C,D) The corresponding MS spectra from z = −1 and z = −2 areas of deprotonated HG species, showing the various detected species upon 13‐m SLIM IMS‐MS (orange) and cyclic IM‐MS (green) experiments.

sec.p: In the IMS heatmap of the 10‐m SLIM IMS experiment (Figure 2A), several ion distributions could be observed with different charge states. Two clear distributions are observed, with the first in the range 200–1000 ms corresponding to the singly charged species and the second being the doubly charged species in the range 100–800 ms. In addition, a congested area is observed corresponding to species with higher charge states (z = −3 and −4). These species are observed with numerous Na+ adducts (replacement of a hydrogen by a sodium on a free carboxylic function).

In the case of cyclic IMS (Figure 2B), two clear distributions are observed, corresponding to the singly charged species in the range 12–80 ms and the doubly charged species in the range 12–60 ms. Here, an area with higher charge states (z < −2) was observed in the range 10–25 ms with no Na+ adducts observed, thus the less congested IMS heatmap.

The difference in ion distributions makes for a less‐than‐ideal evaluation between platforms. The contrast between heatmaps in Figure 2 indicates most of the ion population exists in higher charge states when sampled using Agilent's high flow ion source that equips the SLIM IMS. The complexity of this region due to spectral overlap limits interpretation. From these observations, we focused only on singly and doubly charged species areas in both experiments, for which the MS spectra were extracted separately. While the ion populations in these charge states are more limited in the SLIM platform, it provides the best starting point for comparing systems.

Figure 2C,D shows the obtained MS spectra for each area. Species identified as singly and doubly charged ions are detailed respectively in Table S1 and Table S2. Based on the annotation of the detected ions, certain differences were noted between the nature of the ions detected for each species depending on the instrument used. If the parameters of each instrument were optimized to maximize the detection of a large range of oligosaccharides (OS), the difference between each configuration (ion source, ion transmission, pressure, etc.) can cause these differences. With the cyclic IMS, the ions are exclusively identified as [M‐n.H]n‐ species, while with the 10‐m SLIM, detected ions mainly correspond to Na+ adducts as [M‐n.H + m.Na](n‐m)‐. The second difference concerns the nature of the OS detected. The dispersity of these OS depending on their DP and their DM is summarized in Figure 3 regardless of the nature of the ions (charge state or adducts). The representation clearly addressed that the cyclic IMS allows detection of higher‐methylated species from DP 7 to DP 14 (in green), while SLIM allows detection of lower‐methylated and larger OS up to DP 17 (in orange).

caption.p: Nature of the OS identified on both instruments depending on their DP and their DM.

sec.p: Overall, the MS spectra obtained after one pass (10 m) on the SLIM IMS allowed the identification of 51 OS, while the Cyclic IMS (~1 m) allowed the identification of 39 OS, these species are summarized in Table S3 and Table S4. These numbers do not consider the isomeric forms at this point (i.e., species with the same DP and DM but different methylation patterns along the chain). Interestingly, despite instrumental parameters that affect the nature of the ions detected, this performance is to be compared with that of UPLC–MS analysis of the same sample, where only 27 distinct OS species were identified over a 40 min gradient, without resolving the isomers [36]. These results highlight the capabilities of the two ultra‐high‐resolution ion mobility instruments to outperform UHPLC chromatography by separating a greater number of methylated oligogalacturonans derived from natural HG mixtures in a 40 times faster analysis time (acquisition time < 1 min).

sec.title: A Two‐Pass (~2 m) Cyclic IMS Separation Provides Performance Comparable to a Single‐Pass (10‐M) SLIM IMS for Isomer Discrimination
sec.p: The isomeric structures present in the oligogalacturonan sample arise from the distribution of methyl esterification at the C6 position along the galactosyl backbone (see Figure 1), and from the specificity of the pectin‐lyase used for polysaccharide degradation, which generates oligosaccharides bearing a methyl ester at the non‐reducing end residue [37]. The original motivation of the present study was to evaluate and compare the capacity of both 10‐m SLIM IMS and cyclic IMS to resolve isomeric structures occurring in the oligogalacturonans' sample. An example of such a comparison (i.e., 10‐m SLIM IMS vs. cyclic IMS) is given in Figure 4, from a DP5DM4 oligogalacturonan observed at m/z 935.21 as a deprotonated species [M‐H]−. The left panel shows the ATD for the selected species using the 10‐m SLIM IMS instrument and the right panel shows the cyclic IMS results for the same species, acquired by single‐pass experiment (i.e., 10 m in the SLIM and ~2 m in the cyclic IMS).

caption.p: Arrival time distributions (ATDs) of [DP5DM4‐H]‐ species at m/z 935.21 upon one pass on 10‐m SLIM IMS (A) vs. one pass on the cyclic IMS (B). The results are represented via 2D IMS heatmaps (top panel) and their corresponding mobilograms (bottom panel) that shows two to three gas‐phase structures depending on the used IMS device.

sec.p: In the 10‐m SLIM IMS case, the ATD shows three well‐resolved distinguished conformations at 842.3 ms, 882.1 ms, and 911.7 ms. Theoretical considerations predict four putative isomers for the case of DP5DM4 species (Figure S2), suggesting that the 10‐m SLIM IMS ATD profile reflects the expected isomeric complexity. However, and importantly, without structural confirmation, it remains hypothetic whether these three signals arise from distinct methyl‐ester positional isomers or from different gas‐phase conformers of the same isomeric form, even if the number of features coincides rather well with the expected number of isomers.

For cyclic IMS, only two features were resolved at 38.6 ms and 41.1 ms. These may likewise correspond to either conformers or distinct isomers. In the latter case, the results from cyclic IMS align perfectly with the study of Ropartz et al. [36], where two isomers were identified via UHPLC fractionation and an offline ESI‐VUVPD.

Overall and rather expectedly, 10‐m SLIM IMS provides enhanced separation, revealing additional gas‐phase species that are not resolved by single‐pass (~1 m) cyclic IMS nor by previous LC‐VUVPD analysis; in this case, the improved resolving power also uncovers an additional feature for the DP5DM4 species.
To challenge the two IMS platforms, multi‐pass separations were implemented on the 10‐m SLIM IMS and the cyclic IMS for the DP5DM4 species.

Figure 5 presents the extracted ATDs for [M‐H]−, m/z 935.21 upon two passes using 10‐m SLIM IMS, i.e., 20 m (left panel), vs. 2 passes using cyclic IMS, i.e., ~2 m (right panel). With the 10‐m SLIM IMS device, we once again observe three resolved features at 1679.2 ms, 1761.3 ms and 1821.8 ms, with a slight improvement in the separation resolution than the single‐pass (10 m) experiment (Rp‐p = ~1.6 at one pass to ~2.1 at two passes).

caption.p: Mobilograms of [DP5DM4‐H]− species at m/z 935.21 showing the ATDs upon 1p (top panel) vs. 2p (bottom panel) on the 10‐m SLIM IMS (A) vs. on the cyclic IMS (B). This experiment highlights the benefits of multi‐pass experiment in both IMS devices.

sec.p: With the cyclic IMS device, the number of separated species ramps to three features, observed respectively at 64.8 ms, 69.9 ms, and 69.7 ms. Here, cyclic IMS provided improved separation upon two passes (~2 m), clearly outperforming the single‐pass (~1 m) experiment and rising the number of identified gas‐phase structures up to three major conformations. This aligns with the number of gas‐phase structures identified in single‐pass (10 m) and two‐passes (20 m) experiments for 10‐m SLIM IMS; in contrast, the improvement in separation of DP5DM4 species from one pass to two passes in the SLIM platform remains moderate.

Interestingly, when comparing the time‐based resolving power (Rp, calculated at m/z 731.16) between the one‐pass and two‐passes experiments, the 10‐m SLIM IMS device already outperforms the cyclic IMS device at one pass, reaching a Rp of ~40 compared to only ~20 for the cyclic IMS. Increasing to two passes results in a Rp of ~42 for the 10‐m SLIM IMS (a ×1.05‐fold gain) and ~29 for the Cyclic IMS (a ×1.45‐fold gain). Theoretically, the Rp is expected to scale with √n, where n is the number of passes; thus, cyclic IMS aligns closely with theory, while SLIM remains moderately below theoretical expectations. Although the SLIM showed only a marginal increase in resolving power from one to two passes, it remained far more resolutive than the cyclic, which exhibited a larger relative improvement when performing multi‐pass experiments. Not surprisingly, the SLIM continues to outperform the cyclic because its intrinsic path length and resolving power are significantly higher (Figure S3).

Of note, Rp was calculated from arrival time–based measurements, which inherently favors shorter‐path devices (i.e., favors the cyclic IMS platform). A CCS‐based comparison would provide a more balanced assessment but was not performed here.

The lower‐than‐expected gain in resolving power for the SLIM IMS in multi‐pass mode could be due to issues pertaining to the SLIM module used in this work since previous SLIM modules have shown the expected increase in resolving power in the multi‐pass mode [16].

Future improvements for SLIM IMS may come from systematic adjustment of parameters such as waveform shape, amplitude, and traveling‐wave speed, highlighting a trade‐off between the high separation potential of long‐path SLIM IMS and the operational flexibility of cyclic IMS.

Similar to the previous example with DP5DM4, the number of the conformations observed in the ATD profiles obtained using 10‐m SLIM IMS or cyclic IMS platforms was determined for the diversity of chemical structures detected in the sample. The measurements were performed using one pass on both instruments (i.e., 10 m for SLIM IMS and ~1 m for cyclic IMS), and the results are summarized in Table 1, along with the number of potential structural isomers.

caption.p: Number of putative isomers compared to the identified conformations using 10‐m SLIM IMS and cyclic IMS.

sec.p: When we compare the total number of conformations detected across all species in the −1 and −2 charge states, we find that cyclic IMS generally resolves more features than 10‐m SLIM IMS for the same set of oligogalacturonans, with a total of 184 conformations that were detected using cyclic IMS, compared to 34 using 10‐m SLIM IMS. The difference in the number of features can be rationalized by the fact that only 15 of the 39 species listed in Table 1 are detected in the z = −1/−2 charge state in SLIM. Further, representative ions for a given species are different. In Table 1, only deprotonated species (‐H, ‒2H) are reported for clarity, although some ions detected in SLIM appear as sodium‐adducted forms, which may affect the number of observed features. The presence of Na adducted species can alter the number of observed features for a given species. Notably, for certain species, primarily those with higher degrees of polymerization (DP ≥ 10), conformational features are not observed on the 10‐m SLIM IMS platform (Figure S4) due to their low MS signal. This behavior may result from increased ion diffusion along the longer separation path, limited transmission efficiency under the current parameters, or simply from the intrinsic complexity of the sample leading to signal congestion. In contrast, cyclic IMS allows for more straightforward tuning and optimization of experimental parameters, which likely facilitates the observation of a larger number of isomeric or conformational species present in the mixture. This operational flexibility represents a key practical distinction between the two platforms. Importantly, although a large number of putative isomeric species (up to ~240) may exist for certain species, only a subset is experimentally populated and detected. In this context, the apparent limitations observed with the 10‐m SLIM IMS are more likely associated with the ionization efficiency and ion population effects rather than the intrinsic resolving capabilities, and could be mitigated through further optimization of the ionization process.

Interestingly, in several cases, both 10‐m SLIM IMS and cyclic IMS reveal a greater number of conformational features that may correspond to isomeric species than previously reported by LC–MS. In addition to the DP5DM4 example discussed earlier, the DP3DM2 species is observed as two features in both 10‐m SLIM IMS and cyclic IMS, consistent with the presence of two theoretical structural isomers, whereas LC–MS resolved only a single species. Similarly, LC–MS revealed two isomers for DP4DM3, while cyclic IMS detects two and 10‐m SLIM IMS resolves three distinct features. These specific examples (DP3DM2, DP4DM3, and DP5DM4) illustrate cases in which the extended path length and high resolving power of the 10‐m SLIM IMS enable the separation of additional conformational or isomeric features beyond those accessible by LC–MS and, in some instances, cyclic IMS.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: We demonstrated that two high‐resolution IMS platforms, 10‐m SLIM IMS and cyclic IMS, enable direct, LC‐free, gas‐phase separation of complex natural mixtures of homogalacturonan oligosaccharides (HGOS). In the absence of prior liquid chromatography, both systems effectively reduce spectral congestion, allowing the detection and resolution of a large number of methyl‐esterified oligogalacturonan species.

Differences in the distribution of charge states existed between the two platforms which can be attributed to their different source geometries. The analysis performed here focused on z = −1 and −2 as these ions were observed in both platforms. Unfortunately, because most of the ion intensity on the SLIM platform was biased towards larger m/z and the z = −3 and −4 charge states, the comparison between the two platforms becomes hindered. Differences are further amplified by the fact that most of the ions observed in SLIM are found as sodium adducted species which can influence the number of distributions observed compared to the equivalent non‐sodium species. Barring these differences, we see in single‐pass experiments, 10‐m SLIM IMS provides an advantage in mixture characterization, yielding a slightly greater number of resolved molecular features compared to one‐pass cyclic IMS (~1 m) when comparing the ATD of equivalent species in equivalent charge states and adduction type.

Both IMS platforms also show high‐resolution separation of gas‐phase structures that can be correlated directly with structural isomerism arising from distinct positional methyl‐esterification motifs along the galactosyl backbone. When evaluated for isomer characterization, both systems show complementary strengths; the extended path of 10‐m SLIM improves decongestion of highly complex mixtures and maximizes species coverage, whereas the operational flexibility of cyclic IMS enables detailed, multi‐pass manipulation, providing detailed elucidation of isomeric species.

We further demonstrate that the single‐pass 10‐m SLIM remains more resolutive than the two‐pass cyclic IMS (~2 m), as expected, yet the two‐pass cyclic setup achieves a marked gain relative to its single‐pass mode, underscoring the flexibility and ease of tunability of this instrument.

Altogether, this study highlights the complementarity of 10‐m SLIM and cyclic IMS for advanced structural analysis of natural polysaccharides, with 10‐m SLIM exceling in mixture decongestion and broad molecular coverage and cyclic IMS providing superior flexibility for in‐depth isomeric dissection. These combined capabilities show that both platforms are powerful tools for further applications in carbohydrate structural characterization.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Rania Benazza: writing – original draft, validation, methodology, formal analysis, data curation. Christopher P. Harrilal: conceptualization, data curation, investigation, methodology, resources, writing – original draft preparation. Yehia M. Ibrahim: conceptualization, funding acquisition, project administration, supervision, writing – review and editing. Mathieu Fanuel: methodology, validation, data curation. David Ropartz: conceptualization, funding acquisition, project administration, resources, supervision, writing – review and editing. Hélène Rogniaux: funding acquisition, project administration, supervision, writing – review and editing.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This work was supported by the AMETHYST project of the PEPR DIADEM, with the financial support from the France 2030 program (project number ANR‐22‐PEXD‐0015).

caption.p: Figure S1: Total MS spectra of HGB69 upon its analysis on (A) SLIM IM‐MS and (B) cyclic IM‐MS.
Table S1: Summary table of identified singly charged species in spectra obtained from IMS‐MS analysis via cyclic (blue) and via SLIM (orange).
Table S2: Summary table of identified doubly charged species in spectra obtained from IMS‐MS analysis via cyclic (blue) and via SLIM (orange).
Table S3: Unique identified DP/DM species using cyclic IMS.
Table S4: Unique identified DP/DM species using SLIM.
Figure S2: Oligo‐homogalacturonan structure represented in a DP5DM4 methylated species with the possible isomers.
Figure S3: Mobilograms of [DP4DM2‐H]‐ species at 731.16 m/z showing the ATDs upon 1p vs. 2p on (top panel) the SLIM IMS and (bottom panel) on the cyclic IMS with the calculated Rp at each condition.

Figure S4: Mobilograms of [DP6DM2‐H]‐ species at 1083.22 m/z, [DP6DM3‐H]‐ species at 1097.24 m/z and [DP6DM4‐H]‐ species at 1111.26 m/z showing the ATDs upon 1p on the SLIM IMS (left) and on the cyclic IMS (right).

sec.title: Acknowledgments
sec.p: This work is powered by the AMETHYST project of the PEPR DIADEM, with the financial support from the France 2030 program (project number ANR‒22‐PEXD‐0015). The authors would like to thank Dr. Christopher Anderton for facilitating running the samples in SLIM. The SLIM work was performed in the Environmental Molecular Sciences Laboratory, a DOE OBER national scientific user facility on the PNNL campus. PNNL is a multiprogram national laboratory operated by Battelle for the DOE under contract DE‐AC05‐76RL01830. Open access publication funding provided by COUPERIN CY26.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

mixed-citation.named-content: Ralet M.‐C., Williams M. A. K., Tanhatan‐Nasseri A., Ropartz D., Quemener B., and Bonnin E., “Innovative Enzymatic Approach to Resolve Homogalacturonans Based on Their Methylesterification Pattern,” Biomacromolecules 13, no. 5 (2012): 1615–1624, 10.1021/bm300329r.

ext-link.xlink:href: journal=Biomacromolecules&title=Innovative Enzymatic Approach to Resolve Homogalacturonans Based on Their Methylesterification Pattern&author=M.‐C. Ralet&author=M. A. K. Williams&author=A. Tanhatan‐Nasseri&author=D. Ropartz&author=B. Quemener&volume=13&issue=5&publication_year=2012&pages=1615-1624&pmid=22520025&doi=10.1021/bm300329r&

caption.p: Figure S1: Total MS spectra of HGB69 upon its analysis on (A) SLIM IM‐MS and (B) cyclic IM‐MS.
Table S1: Summary table of identified singly charged species in spectra obtained from IMS‐MS analysis via cyclic (blue) and via SLIM (orange).
Table S2: Summary table of identified doubly charged species in spectra obtained from IMS‐MS analysis via cyclic (blue) and via SLIM (orange).
Table S3: Unique identified DP/DM species using cyclic IMS.
Table S4: Unique identified DP/DM species using SLIM.
Figure S2: Oligo‐homogalacturonan structure represented in a DP5DM4 methylated species with the possible isomers.
Figure S3: Mobilograms of [DP4DM2‐H]‐ species at 731.16 m/z showing the ATDs upon 1p vs. 2p on (top panel) the SLIM IMS and (bottom panel) on the cyclic IMS with the calculated Rp at each condition.

Figure S4: Mobilograms of [DP6DM2‐H]‐ species at 1083.22 m/z, [DP6DM3‐H]‐ species at 1097.24 m/z and [DP6DM4‐H]‐ species at 1111.26 m/z showing the ATDs upon 1p on the SLIM IMS (left) and on the cyclic IMS (right).

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
